Nucleosomes facilitate their own invasion Gu Li & Jonathan Widom DNA wrapped in nucleosomes is sterically occluded, creating obstacles for polymerase, regulatory, remodeling, repair and recombination complexes, which require access to the wrapped DNA. How such complexes recognize and gain access to their DNA target sites is not known. Here we report the direct detection of a dynamic equilibrium conformational transition in nucleosomes that greatly increases the distance between the end of the nucleosomal DNA and the histone core. We quantified the equilibrium constant for this transition under physiological conditions. As predicted by these findings, addition of LexA protein to nucleosomes containing the LexA target site drives this conformational equilibrium toward the unwrapped, accessible state, simultaneously allowing stable LexA binding. This inherent property of nucleosomes allows any protein, whether an energy-dependent machine or a passive binder, to gain access even to buried stretches of nucleosomal DNA.

Genomic DNA in eukaryotes is organized in a repeating array of nucleosomes in which short stretches of DNA are wrapped twice around spools of histone proteins1. DNA wrapped in nucleosomes is sterically occluded even from small proteins such as DNAseI, yet the entire genome must be replicated by large DNA polymerases, and nucleosome-wrapped genes must be transcribed by large RNA polymerases and function as substrate for large remodeling, repair and recombination complexes. Many regulatory proteins cannot bind to DNA target sites wrapped inside a nucleosome, or cannot bind unless their target sites have a precise rotational register on the histone surface, which often is not the case2. Nucleosomal DNA needs to be unwrapped at times to carry out its biological function3–7. How this unwrapping is accomplished is not known. Access to buried sites in nucleosomes can be facilitated by ATPdependent remodeling factors that actively move or unwrap nucleosomes4,6,8–11. Such factors might accompany polymerases during replication or transcription; they might act on nucleosomes ubiquitously throughout the genome; or they might be recruited to act on specific nucleosomes. However, ATP-dependent remodeling factors may not always be required to permit access to buried target sites3,5,12–15. Early studies have shown that exonucleases and bacteriophage RNA polymerases can access the several outermost base pairs of the nucleosome core particle DNA, suggesting that the outermost base pairs of the nucleosomal DNA might transiently release from the histone surface16. Subsequent studies have shown that passive DNA-binding proteins and restriction enzymes can bind to their target sites even when the sites are buried far inside nucleosomes12,13,17,18 or polynucleosomes19,20. The detailed mechanisms and conformational changes underlying such spontaneous (uncatalyzed) site accessibility are not known. An early study21 concluded that transient accessibility of buried target sites occurred as a consequence of a previously discovered inherent ability of

nucleosomes to translocate along the DNA that wraps them22: buried stretches of the nucleosomal DNA would be accessible during transient periods when the histone octamer had diffused along the DNA to a new location. However, a subsequent study23 tested this conclusion directly and showed it to be incorrect. This study suggested instead that spontaneous access to buried sites must occur by some intramolecular conformational change in the nucleosome that exposes a stretch of the nucleosomal DNA off the histone octamer while the histone octamer remains fixed in place on the remaining wrapped DNA. Although this newer study defined one mechanism by which nucleosomal target sites are not made accessible, it provided no mechanistic detail for how sites actually are made accessible. Possibilities that have been suggested include a transient partial unwrapping of the wrapped nucleosomal DNA starting from one end12, or the formation of stable bulged structures in which the ends of the nucleosomal DNA remain wrapped13. Bulged structures could arise by many mechanisms24–26, and have also been suggested to represent key stable products of nucleosome remodeling9,10. The present study was undertaken to define the actual nature of these spontaneous conformational changes in the nucleosome. In fact, these two broad mechanisms for accessing DNA that is wrapped in nucleosomes—via ATP-dependent remodeling factors or via passive binding—are not exclusive. Here we report experiments showing that nucleosomes inherently facilitate both of these mechanisms through spontaneous large-scale conformational changes that involve the transient partial unwrapping of the wrapped nucleosomal DNA. In addition, we quantify the equilibrium constant for dynamic unwrapping in physiological conditions; we show that nucleosomes shift their conformational equilibrium in response to the simple presence of an exogenous site–specific DNA-binding protein; and we show that the major conformational changes in the nucleosome coupled to protein binding occur only at the DNA end nearest to the site where the protein binds.

Quantitative studies discussed below establish that the FRET efficiency in these solution conditions is ∼100%; residual D emission remains because the labeling by A is incomplete. Partial unwrapping of the DNA would increase R (measured to either copy of A) in both FRET systems, and thus would decrease the FRET efficiency. We tested whether considerable unwrapping occurs in solutions at physiological ionic strength by titrating nucleosomes with increasing concentrations of NaCl ([NaCl]) (Fig. 2). Concentrated NaCl serves as a positive control, as ∼0.75–1 M NaCl quantitatively displaces H2A–H2B heterodimers, whereas ∼1–1.5 M displaces H32H42 tetramers29. We observed [NaCl]dependent decreases in FRET efficiency not just at [NaCl] ≥ 0.75 M, but also through the physiological range of [NaCl] (Fig. 2a,b). Figure 2 Dynamic equilibrium unwrapping of nucleosomal DNA in physiological [NaCl]. (a) Titrations of H2A K119C DA-labeled nucleosomes with [NaCl]. Increasing [NaCl] leads to increased D emission and decreased A emission (arrows), indicating a [NaCl]-dependent loss of FRET efficiency (E). For [NaCl] ≥ 1.75 M, histones are quantitatively displaced from the DNA, and E = 0. (b) Equivalent [NaCl] titration of H3 V35C (C110A) nucleosomes. (c) [NaCl] titration of D-only nucleosomes. (d) Relative FRET efficiency (E/E0) for the two systems as functions of cation concentration ([Na+ ] + [Tris+]), from the data of a–c. (e) Expanded scale representation of data from d and additional experiments. Data points: mean ± 1 σ. (f) Equilibrium constants (Keq; mean ± 1 σ, n = 3) for partial DNA unwrapping as a function of cation concentration, calculated from the data in d,e and additional independent titrations. Keq is ∼0.02–0.1 at physiological ionic strength (∼0.1–0.15 M ([Na+ ] + [Tris+])). This means that for nucleosomes in physiological conditions, a given nucleosome is considerably but partially unwrapped ∼2–10% of the time.

of the histone tails. The label on histone H3 V35C was placed at a point on the H3 tail just beyond where the tail was fixed in place between the two DNA gyres (Fig. 1b), hence, large-scale motions at this residue are not possible—at least while the DNA remains wrapped. The two different systems exhibited equivalent FRET changes, but identical [NaCl]-dependent structural transitions of the two different labeled proteins would not be expected. Finally, closely related structural changes detected by FRET in an experiment described below cannot be explained by [NaCl]-dependent changes to the histone tails because the experiment was carried out at constant [NaCl]. We conclude that nucleosomes exhibit [NaCl]-dependent conformational changes involving a partial unwrapping of the DNA off the histone core, starting from an end of the wrapped DNA. Changes in the DNA wrapping could depend on [NaCl] in this ∼0–0.5 M range by either of two different mechanisms. One possibility is that, at a given [NaCl], all nucleosomes have identical conformations. In this case, the interpretation of the FRET data would be that increasing [NaCl] results in a gradually increasing amount of DNA unwrapped off the nucleosome surface, with a corresponding gradual increase in R monitored by FRET. An alternative possibility is that each nucleosome could exist in a dynamic conformational equilibrium between two states, one fully wrapped (small R, large E), the other achieved by large-scale partial unwrapping (large R, small E). In this case, the interpretation of the FRET experiment would be that FRET measures the time-averaged distribution of the population between these two states. This population distribution could be described by an equilibrium constant, Keq, the ratio of the probability of unwrapped states to the probability of the fully wrapped state.

The [NaCl] dependence on the FRET efficiency would then be due to a [NaCl] dependence on Keq itself. Classical studies in chromatin molecular biology and in DNA physical chemistry refute the first explanation and support the second. A uniform partial DNA unwrapping under physiological conditions is inconsistent with the well-known resistance of the nucleosome core DNA to digestion by diverse nucleases29. Conversely, the affinities of protein-DNA interactions are well known to depend strongly on the solution cation concentration30. We conclude that the reduced FRET efficiency measured under physiological conditions is a result of a dynamic conformational equilibrium between wrapped and unwrapped states, and that the equilibrium depends on [NaCl]. A minimal description requires two states—fully wrapped and unwrapped—and is described by a single [NaCl]-dependent equilibrium constant. We analyzed the data from Figure 2d,e and additional independent titrations to yield Keq as a function of [NaCl] (Fig. 2f). The measured Keq values are ∼0.02–0.1 over a relatively broad range of [NaCl] around physiological ionic strength (∼0.1–0.15 M NaCl), meaning that, for nucleosomes in physiological conditions, a given nucleosome is considerably but partially unwrapped ∼2–10% of the time. Earlier studies of site accessibility in nucleosomes included Mg2+ in the solutions12,23,31,32. To relate our new experiments to those studies we used FRET to monitor changes arising when nucleosomes in 0.1 M NaCl were titrated with 0–10 mM MgCl2 (Supplementary Fig. 1 online). In the presence of 0.1 M Na+, addition of 0–10 mM Mg2+ did not substantially change the equilibrium constant for unwrapping. Thus, the new FRET measurements of Keq can be compared with earlier measurements of the fractional accessibility of DNA target sites.

(Fig. 4). LexA binding to the naked 147-L DNA (Fig. 4a) yielded a discrete gel shift and occurred cooperatively, with an EC50 of ∼5 nM. As expected, equivalent results were obtained with DNA 147-R (Fig. 4c). Binding to the 147-L nucleosomes (Fig. 4b) and 147-R nucleosomes (Fig. 4d) did not yield a detectable gel shift until very high [LexA], much greater than the EC50 for binding to 147-L nucleosomes observed by FRET (Fig. 3e). The site-specifically bound LexA– nucleosome complex lacks sufficient stability to survive native gel electrophoresis. These results confirm additional aspects of the interpretation of the FRET studies. First, as predicted by the site exposure model12, the apparent affinity for binding of LexA to a nucleosomal site (measured by FRET, Fig. 3e) is decreased by ∼10–100-fold relative to the affinity for a naked DNA site (estimated from the gel shift, Fig. 4a,b). Second, LexA binding is saturating at the highest [LexA] analyzed by FRET. Third, nucleosomes remain intact even after LexA is bound (for example, when [LexA] exceeds the EC50 for binding).

Figure 4 Native gel analysis for LexA binding to naked DNA and nucleosomes. (a) Native gel mobility shift assay for LexA binding to naked DNA 147-L in the same solution conditions used for FRET analysis. 147-L DNA, mobility of free DNA probe; DNA-LexA, set of bands corresponding to DNA-LexA complexes having varying numbers of bound LexA molecules. 100-bp ladder is included as size standard. The system exhibits cooperative binding with a transition midpoint (EC50) between 3 and 10 nM. (b) Native gel mobility shift assay for LexA binding to nucleosomes containing 147-L DNA, in the same solution conditions. 147-L DNA, mobility of contaminating free DNA (present in negligibly small amounts); 147-L nucleosome, mobility of purified nucleosomes; nucleosome–LexA, set of bands corresponding to nucleosome–LexA complexes having varying numbers of bound LexA molecules. 100-bp ladder is included as size standard. Specific LexA–nucleosome complexes present at [LexA] = 300 nM (Fig. 3b,e) do not survive native gel electrophoresis. The results show that LexA binding is saturating at the highest [LexA] analyzed by FRET, and that nucleosomes remain intact when even [LexA] exceeds the EC50 for binding detected by FRET. (c) Native gel analysis for binding of LexA to naked 147-R DNA; conditions as in a. As expected, the inherent affinity of LexA for 147-R DNA is indistinguishable from that for 147-L. (d) Native gel analysis for binding to nucleosomes containing 147-R DNA; conditions as in b.

DISCUSSION Our new results, and many aspects of the behavior of nucleosomes observed in earlier studies, can be understood with the model shown in Figure 5. Fully wrapped nucleosomes (i) are in dynamic equilibrium with a set of partially unwrapped states (ii). More extensive unwrapping fluctuations incur a greater energetic cost and have a correspondingly lower probability. In the presence of appropriate site-specific DNA-binding proteins, the system responds by driving the equilibrium toward the unwrapped state, coupled to binding of the protein (iii). The histone octamer remains fixed in place along the remaining wrapped DNA. Processive enzymes may progress unhindered along unwrapped DNA, and may progress further through the nucleosome as additional stretches of DNA in front of the enzymes unwrap transiently. Our work establishes that nucleosomes facilitate their own invasion. Large-scale DNA unwrapping transitions occur spontaneously under physiological conditions and are markedly prevalent at equilibrium. ATP-dependent nucleosome remodeling factors could act as Brownian ratchets33 to harness these spontaneous fluctuations, or the factors could actively facilitate these conformational fluctuations by applying force9–11. In either case, nucleosomes facilitate the remodeling factor’s activity by being inherently poised for large-scale DNA unwrapping. Nucleosomes also facilitate their own invasion by passive DNA-binding proteins, as the spontaneous unwrapping fluctuations detected here make all regions of the nucleosomal DNA transiently accessible3,5,12–15. Earlier studies of protein binding to polynucleosomes show that the ability of proteins to bind buried target sites is not an ‘end effect,’ but it applies also to nucleosomes in the middle of longer chains19,20. As a corollary, partial DNA unwrapping mechanically links exposure of a DNA target site with the exposure of other target sites that are distal along the DNA. This linkage leads to cooperative invasion and combi-

natorial control of the nucleosome by two or more independent, passive DNA-binding proteins, in vitro13 and in vivo15, through collaborative competition with the histone octamer. For the particular case of LexA protein, our experimental results and model account for the widespread ability of fusion proteins containing the LexA DNAbinding domain to bind to, and act appropriately at, LexA target sites that are integrated at diverse genomic locations, in vivo, in many different eukaryotic cell types and organisms. Our model also accounts for the selective recruitment of ATPdependent remodeling factors to specific nucleosomes by passive DNA-binding regulatory proteins that identify the particular nucleosomes destined to be remodeled. At present, it is not known how remodeling factors are recruited to particular nucleosomes. The available evidence implies that remodeling factors are often recruited to specific nucleosomes by site-specific binding factors8,34, but how these site-specific binding factors are able to find and bind to their target sites is not known. Even when nucleosomes are highly positioned, a substantial fraction of cells in a population have nucleosomes in locations that differ from the mode2, leaving critical binding sites in locations or orientations that do not allow for binding without concomitant changes in nucleosome structure. Our results suggest that site-specific DNA-binding proteins may recruit remodeling

[Page 6]

Histone octamer and LexA preparation. Xenopus laevis recombinant histone proteins were prepared as described37. Histone mutations were prepared using the QuikChange site-directed mutagenesis kit (Stratagene). The naturally occurring histone H3 variant H3 C110A was used in place of wild-type H3 in all studies. Two new histone mutations were generated: H3 V35C (C110A) and H2A K119C. H3 V35C and H2A K119C are both in solvent-exposed regions of histone tails, just outside the wrapped DNA, thus dyes attached at these locations are expected not to interfere with the folded protein architecture or with DNA wrapping. Altogether, six histones were expressed and purified: H2A, H2A K119C, H2B, H3 C110A, H3 V35C (C110A), and H4. Desired sets of the four histones were mixed at equal molarity and refolded to form two types of histone octamer: (H2A, H2B, H3 V35C (C110A), H4); and (H2A K119C, H2B, H3 C110A, H4). LexA protein was prepared from expression plasmid pJWL228 (gift of J. Little, University of Arizona, Tucson) and purified to near-homogeneity as described38.

Figure 5 Model linking equilibrium conformational fluctuations of nucleosomes to nucleosome activity. Fully wrapped nucleosomes (i) are in dynamic equilibrium with a set of partially unwrapped states (ii). In the presence of appropriate site-specific binding proteins, the system responds by driving the equilibrium toward the unwrapped state, coupled to binding of the protein (iii). Conformational fluctuations from state (i) to state (ii) are spontaneous, inherent properties of nucleosomes, and are remarkably prevalent: nucleosomes spend ∼2–10% of the time in such highly altered conformations under physiological conditions. This inherent behavior of nucleosomes allows any protein, whether an energy-dependent machine or a passive binder, to gain access even to buried stretches of nucleosomal DNA.

factors to particular nucleosomes during moments when the DNA target sites are exposed spontaneously. Once recruited to a particular nucleosome, a remodeling factor can move or disassemble that nucleosome, eliminating it as a competitor, thereby allowing the site-specific binding protein to bind with much greater equilibrium occupancy than would be possible were the nucleosome to have remained a competitor. In this way, regulatory protein binding followed by remodeling factor action can make occupancy by the regulatory factor much more binary, or switch-like, in character than is possible with simple binding to a freely exposed regulatory DNA target site. METHODS DNA preparation. DNA constructs were generated by PCR using the selected high-affinity nucleosome positioning sequence 601 (ref. 35) as template. DNA sequence 147 represents the 147-bp sequence of the dominant nucleosome position36 on the 282-bp sequence 601. For construct 147, PCR primers were as follows. Left-end primer, Cy3-LE19: Cy3-CTGGAGAATCCCGGTGCCG; right-end primer, RE23: ACAGGATGTATATATCTGACACG. Construct 147-L was built in two stages of PCR. The first stage introduced a 20-bp binding site for LexA beginning at nucleotide 8 of sequence 147, whereas the second stage introduced the Cy3 dye on the left-hand 5′ end. PCR primers were as follows. First generation, left-end primer, LE47: CTGGAGATACTGTATGAGCATACAGTACAATTGGTCGTAGCAAGCTC (LexA-binding sequence is underlined); right-end primer, RE23 (see above). Second generation, left-end primer, Cy3-LE24: Cy3-CTGGAGATACTGTATGAGCATACA; right-end primer, RE23. For construct 147-R, the 20-bp LexA-binding site was engineered at the symmetry-equivalent location on the right hand side of sequence 147. PCR primers were as follows. Left-end primer, Cy3-LE19 (see above); right-end primer, RE47: ACAGGATTACTGTATGAGCATACAGTATGGAGACTAGGGA GTAATCC (LexA-binding sequence underlined). PCR products were further purified by reverse-phase HPLC on a ZORBAX column (Agilent).

Cy5 modification and nucleosome reconstitution. Refolded histone octamers were reduced with TCEP and labeled with Cy5 maleimide as recommended (Amersham Pharmacia Biotech) in 2 M NaCl, 20 mM HEPES, pH 7.3. Cy5labeled histone octamers were mixed with DNAs at octamer/DNA molar ratios of 0.8:1 in 2 M NaCl. D-only nucleosomes were prepared with histone octamer comprising H2A, H2B, H3 V35C (C110A) and H4 but with no Cy5 (A) dye. Nucleosomes were reconstituted by salt gradient dialysis and purified by sucrose gradient ultracentrifugation as described12. Purified nucleosomes were stored in 0.5× TE (TE is 10 mM Tris, pH 7.5, 1 mM EDTA). Fluorescence measurements. Fluorescence experiments were carried out at room temperature (∼23 °C) on a photon-counting steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (ISS PC1) with an additional 550-nm cut-on filter in the emission path. The Cy3 donor dye in DA (doubly labeled) or D (donor-only labeled) nucleosome samples was excited at 515 nm. Emission spectra were collected from 500 to 750 nm. The Cy5 acceptor dye in DA nucleosome samples was directly excited at 610 nm, allowing acceptor-only emission spectra to be collected. Nucleosome concentrations were 8 nM throughout. FRET efficiency from sensitized emission of the acceptor. FRET efficiencies (E) in Figure 3 were measured from the sensitized emission of the acceptor (A), using the (ratio)A method27: E ≈ 2 × [(εA(ν′′) FA(ν′) / FA(ν′′)) − εA(ν′)] / [εD(ν′) d+]. ν′ is the wavelength chosen for excitation of D, 515 nm; ν′′ is the wavelength chosen for direct excitation of A, 610 nm. FA(ν′) is the fluorescence emission from A when excited at 515 nm (after subtraction of any overlapping D emission). FA(ν′′) is the fluorescence emission of A when excited directly at 610 nm. εA(ν′′) is the extinction coefficient of A at 610 nm; εA(ν′) is the extinction coefficient of A at 515 nm; εD(ν′) is the extinction coefficient of D at 515 nm. d+ is the fractional labeling of D, which we take to be 1, as the DNA was prepared by PCR with purified Cy3-labeled primers. The factor of 2 in the equation reflects the presence of about two molecules of A per nucleosome, one of which dominates the FRET. Extinction coefficients of Cy3 and Cy5 at the absorption maxima were provided by the manufacturer. Extinction coefficients at other wavelengths were measured relative to these values, as adducts of the dyes to β-mercaptoethanol, in 0.5× TE buffer. The equation for E is approximate because the fraction of labeling by A is <1 and the extinction coefficients are not accurately known. We find that E = 1.0 for both doubly labeled nucleosome systems in 0.5× TE buffer. If the labeling by A were complete, and E = 1.0, then no D emission would be detected; yet, residual D emission was observed in both systems (Figs. 1c, 2a,b and 3b,c). We conclude that the labeling by A is <100%. Based on the fraction of D emission remaining, we estimate that the fractional labeling by A is ∼75–80%. MALDI and electrospray mass spectral analyses of the labeled histone octamer preparations resolve the mass shifts arising from coupling to Cy5 and, although qualitative, are consistent with this conclusion (G.L., H. Tims, and J.W., data not shown). Relative FRET efficiency from donor emission. For the NaCl titration experiments (Fig. 2), we took advantage of a special feature of this system that allows a simpler and more robust measurement of relative changes in E, based on emission of D only. The apparent energy transfer efficiency E = 1 – ((D emission from DA-labeled nucleosomes) / (D emission from D-only nucleosomes)) ×

((concentration of D-only nucleosomes) / (concentration of DA-labeled nucleosomes)). The apparent E will differ from the absolute transfer efficiency if the labeling by D or A is incomplete. For given preparations of DA-labeled and D-only nucleosomes, we obtained the ratio of their concentrations from measurements of D emission at identical [NaCl] ≥ 1.75 M, which drives all (A-labeled) histones off the DNA, eliminating all FRET. Then, for the [NaCl] titrations, we measured at each [NaCl] the ratio (D emission from DA-labeled nucleosomes) / (D-emission from D-only nucleosomes) from the corresponding DA-labeled (Fig. 2a,b) and D-only (Fig. 2c) samples. Finally, because the E measured by this method is an apparent E, not the absolute E, we measured the relative change in E: we divided the apparent E by E0, the apparent E measured in 0.5× TE + 0 M NaCl, in which the nucleosomes are strongly stabilized. Keq from FRET measurements. The relative FRET efficiency (E / E0) in Figure 2d,e (obtained here using the donor-only method) equals the equilibrium probability (P) that the nucleosomes are in the native, fully wrapped state. P varies with increasing [NaCl] between the value 1, in subphysiological [NaCl] (nucleosomes stabilized, strong FRET), and 0, in concentrated NaCl (nucleosomes fully dissociated, no FRET). Keq was calculated from Keq = (1 − P) / P. Distance estimate from FRET. The actual distance between donor and acceptor (R) can be calculated from the measured energy transfer efficiency (E) and the characteristic transfer distance (R0) for the particular dye pair27, according to E = 1 / (1 + (R / R0)6). R0 itself depends on several parameters27, some of which are difficult to estimate; these include the orientation parameter, the index of refraction of the medium between the dyes, and the extinction coefficients of the dyes themselves. Using the best available estimates for these parameters, R0 has been estimated for the Cy3-Cy5 dye pair to be ∼6 nm (ref. 28). Gel mobility shift assays. Tracer quantities of DNA were labeled at 5′ ends with [γ-32P]ATP using T4 polynucleotide kinase. Some was kept for use as naked DNA, and some was mixed with unlabeled DNA and reconstituted into nucleosomes. Naked DNA at 1 nM, or nucleosomes at 8 nM, was titrated with increasing concentrations of purified LexA in 0.5× TE at room temperature. Samples were equilibrated for 2 min and then loaded onto running 5% (w/v) polyacrylamide native gels. Gels were dried and analyzed by phosphorimager.
